result.markdown:
--- title: "Cognitive Health in the U.S.: An Ecological Study of Alzheimer’s Dementia Prevalence and Potential Risk Factors" format: html editor: visual --- # Method cognitive decline module was chosen to be compared with prevalence of AD. The module in this dataset has 4 topics about self-reported cognitive decline and each topic has one question. A detailed description of the questions is provided in the Result page. By using Dataset name $$State Prevalence=\frac{\sum_{i} (p_i \times n_i)}{\sum_{i} n_i}$$ Where: $p_i$: prevalence in county $i$ $N_i$: population aged ≥65 in county $i$ ## Methods This study integrates county-level Alzheimer’s dementia prevalence data with state-level survey data and U.S. Census demographic data to investigate geographic and socioeconomic patterns associated with cognitive health in the United States. The primary outcome, Alzheimer’s dementia prevalence, was obtained at the county level and aggregated to the state level using a population-weighted mean: $$ \text{State Prevalence} = \frac{\sum_{i} (p_i \times n_i)}{\sum_{i} n_i} $$ where $p_i$ represents the prevalence in county $i$, and $n_i$ denotes the population aged 65 years and older in county $i$. This approach ensures that counties with larger elderly populations contribute proportionally to state-level estimates. Subjective cognitive decline (SCD) was assessed using the Cognitive Decline module from the Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS). Analyses were restricted to overall age-adjusted groups, and comparisons were made between high-prevalence states (Maryland, New York, Mississippi, and Florida) and national estimates. Additional county-level demographic and socioeconomic variables from the U.S. Census were incorporated to explore potential associations with Alzheimer’s dementia prevalence, including housing characteristics and SNAP participation. ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## Results ### 1. Exploratory Data Analysis #### 1.1. Variable-Level Missing Value Analysis To assess data completeness and variables in the Alzheimer’s Disease and Healthy Aging Dataset(AD_dataset), a summary table of missing values of each variable was generated (Table 1). Missing values (NAs) observed in the data_value and confidence limit variables primarily reflect underlying statistical or sampling limitations. Detailed explanations for these missing values are provided in the data_value_footnote_symbol and data_value_footnote variables. The first level of stratification contains no missing values. In contrast, most missingness arises within the second level of stratification, particularly for observations classified as “Overall” within the age group (first stratification). The missing values are intentionally introduced to prevent duplication of information across stratification levels. The missingness in geolocation is for United State category which does not need the location coordination. In summary, the dataset is suitable for analysis. Missing(%) == 0 ~ "Complete", Missing(%) < 20 ~ "High Integrity", Missing(%) < 50 ~ "Conditional" ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}

````` ::: ::: #### 1.2. Data Integrity and Module Summary AD dataset consists of seven modules of questions and responses related to Alzheimer's Disease (AD) or its prevention. The number of observation and the missing data% was calculated and listed (Table 2). The frequency of responses are stratified first by age group, then, gender and race/ethnicity, secondly. The observations are from state-level group data from two age groups(50-64, 65+), and observations named overall stands for data from combined two groups. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Table 2: Data Integrity and Module Summary
Module Observations Missing Values (%) Status
Caregiving 25493 32.3 Retained
Cognitive Decline 22182 36.3 Retained
Mental Health 22184 34.6 Retained
Nutrition/Physical Activity/Obesity 33194 29.8 Retained
Overall Health 96753 29.8 Retained
Screenings and Vaccines 62153 32.7 Retained
Smoking and Alcohol Use 22183 37.3 Retained
````` ::: ::: #### 1.3. Survey questions in Cognitive Decline Module I focused on Cognitive Decline Module because it has frequency of subjective cognitive decline (SCD) (QuestionID 30), which can be an indicator for early cognitive impairment. The Cognitive Decline Module has four questions about SCD frequency (Q30), impact for daily activity (Q31), demend for assistance (Q41), and experience of medical help (Q42) (Table 3). ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Table 3: Mapping of Question ID to Topic and Survey Question
Question ID Summary Topic Survey Question
Q30 Subjective cognitive decline (SCD) freq Subjective cognitive decline or memory loss among older adults Percentage of older adults who reported subjective cognitive decline or memory loss that is happening more often or is getting worse in the preceding 12 months
Q31 Daily activity impact Functional difficulties associated with subjective cognitive decline or memory loss among older adults Percentage of older adults who reported subjective cognitive decline or memory loss that interferes with their ability to engage in social activities or household chores
Q41 Need assistance Need assistance with day-to-day activities because of subjective cognitive decline or memory loss Percentage of older adults who reported that as a result of subjective cognitive decline or memory loss that they need assistance with day-to-day activities
Q42 Discussed with doctor Talked with health care professional about subjective cognitive decline or memory loss Percentage of older adults with subjective cognitive decline or memory loss who reported talking with a health care professional about it
````` ::: ::: #### 1.4. Data Integrity of survey questions in Cognitive Decline Module The Figure 1. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](result_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: #### 2. The frequency of self-reported cognition decline (SDC) in the US states with high Alzheimer's dementia prevalence ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](result_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-6-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: Top 10 SCD questionare states ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](result_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-7-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: pi chart age group ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](result_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-8-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](result_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-10-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](result_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-11-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: USA Mountain map Population density ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Load raw file (no skipping yet) pop_raw <- read_csv("totalpop2020.csv", col_names = TRUE, show_col_types = FALSE) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` New names: • `` -> `...4` ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Check names (IMPORTANT for debugging) #print(names(pop_raw)) # Select columns by position (safe approach) pop_clean <- pop_raw %>% select(2, 3) %>% # 2 = Geographic Area Name, 3 = Total population rename( location = 1, population = 2 ) %>% filter(!is.na(location)) %>% # remove junk rows mutate( population = as.numeric(str_remove_all(population, ",")), location = str_trim(location) ) %>% separate(location, into = c("county", "state"), sep = ", ") %>% mutate( county = str_remove(county, " County"), county_lower = tolower(county), state_lower = tolower(state) ) %>% select(county_lower, state_lower, population) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Warning: There was 1 warning in `mutate()`. ℹ In argument: `population = as.numeric(str_remove_all(population, ","))`. Caused by warning: ! NAs introduced by coercion ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Warning: Expected 2 pieces. Missing pieces filled with `NA` in 1 rows [1]. ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} us_counties <- map_data("county") map_pop <- us_counties %>% left_join(pop_clean, by = c("subregion" = "county_lower", "region" = "state_lower")) p1<- ggplot(map_pop, aes(long, lat, group = group)) + geom_polygon(aes(fill = population), color = NA) + scale_fill_gradient( trans = "log10", low = "#f2e5ff", high = "#4b0082", name = "Population (log scale)", na.value = "grey90" ) + coord_fixed(1.3) + labs(title = "US County Population (2020)") + theme_void() ``` ::: # Covid ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` New names: • `` -> `...14` ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Warning: There was 1 warning in `mutate()`. ℹ In argument: `senior_pop = as.numeric(str_remove_all(senior_pop, ","))`. Caused by warning: ! NAs introduced by coercion ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Warning: Expected 2 pieces. Missing pieces filled with `NA` in 1 rows [1]. ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](result_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-14-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: # Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) There is an association at the county level This is NOT causal inference. Statistically, you are: exploring → scatter plot quantifying → correlation modeling → linear regression Your analysis is: cross-sectional ecological (county-level) So you cannot conclude: SNAP causes Alzheimer’s Only: There is an association at the county level ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` New names: • `` -> `...459` ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](result_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-15-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Prepare data scatter_data <- snap_clean %>% left_join(ad_prevalence, by = c("county_lower", "state_lower")) %>% mutate( prevalence = as.numeric(prevalence), label = paste0( "County: ", county_lower, "
State: ", state_lower, "
% SNAP: ", round(pct_snap, 2), "
AD Prevalence: ", round(prevalence, 2) ) ) %>% filter(!is.na(pct_snap), !is.na(prevalence)) # ========================= # Fit linear model manually # ========================= model <- lm(prevalence ~ pct_snap, data = scatter_data) # Create prediction line x_seq <- seq(min(scatter_data$pct_snap), max(scatter_data$pct_snap), length.out = 100) line_data <- data.frame( pct_snap = x_seq, prevalence = predict(model, newdata = data.frame(pct_snap = x_seq)) ) # Correlation cor_val <- cor(scatter_data$pct_snap, scatter_data$prevalence, use = "complete.obs") # ========================= # Build plot # ========================= p <- ggplot(scatter_data, aes(x = pct_snap, y = prevalence, text = label)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.6) + # 🔥 MANUAL regression line (works in plotly) geom_line(data = line_data, aes(x = pct_snap, y = prevalence), color = "blue", linewidth = 1, inherit.aes = FALSE) + labs( x = "% SNAP Households", y = "AD Prevalence (%)", title = "% SNAP Households vs Alzheimer's Prevalence" ) + theme_minimal() # Convert to interactive + annotation ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text") %>% layout( annotations = list( x = max(scatter_data$pct_snap), y = max(scatter_data$prevalence), text = paste0("r = ", round(cor_val, 3)), showarrow = FALSE, xanchor = "right", yanchor = "top" ) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
